item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced, and therefore must be assumed as non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: psst. This is over 200 days old

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What an intensely terrible and unhelpful comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You should really re-check the whole thing and correct the many many weird collocations, bad grammar, unnatural uses and downright surreal sentences that fill up this piece.

You might want to give an example or several of this. Given how unnecessarily vehement your response of "It may actually be the worse writing I have ever seen in a skip in the four years or more that I've been reading them." comes off as compared to the other comments in the thread, it would be good to put some sort of specific causes of your ire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The English in this one is terrible. It may actually be the worse writing I have ever seen in a skip in the four years or more that I've been reading them. I don't intend to sound disrespectful or rude, I just want you to understand just how much I disliked the writing itself, so sorry if you feel hurt or offended by what I said. You should really re-check the whole thing and correct the many many weird collocations, bad grammar, unnatural uses and downright surreal sentences that fill up this piece. If you are interested I can re-read it and highlight every iffy thing I find for you. Just let me know.

Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! Glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just saw this. Sorry for the long delay in response.

The watch is meant to indicate the amount of time left before reaching an exit, which is what the title meant. Henry would have walked the same length of time with or without the watch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't pinpoint why, but this is my favorite article. Something about the idea of a nigh-endless path with all the time in the world to complete is cool to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok… One question here.

What if the watch counts to 0?

Edit: still not answered yet…
Oh well. I can wait for 80 hours I suppose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Correct me if I'm wrong, but Henry is the only person who is suspect to exit SCP-2503 through SCP-2503-2, right? If the exit has been used only one time (as far as Foundation knows) and the one who made that exit was found dead, then how can we prove that SCP-2503-2 is truly an exit? His corpse could have been just teleported to that point after his death inside SCP-2503. No one saw him open the exit door and come out alive.
Or is the GG counter enough proof that the path connects -1 and -2?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's not suited for me to comment directly as that will interfere with how others interpret this tale, but I will say that reading your theory made me produce a few approving nods here and there (but not everywhere). Thank you for your keen interest and support!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The journal absolutely worked for me. The sheer scale of this thing is something you're not going to understand by looking at a single number; Henry's account of his experience is a much more effective way of communicating what it could do to a person.

+ I have a theory about the kids.
- Let me know if I'm close...

Toward the end of the journal Henry mentions that he's learned how to keep himself from thinking about certain things, particularly memories, to keep from falling into a cycle of despair.

When he finds the drone, he can remember that he had a college roommate who would've been excited about it, but he can't remember the roommate's name.

Henry can't remember his child's name, either. Nor does he remember skipping pub night for "some time to himself."

"I thought the paper was written by me at first because of the handwriting but I don't recall ever writing anything like this."

Conclusion: The journal entries the Foundation recovered were written starting well after Henry found himself in SCP-2503. The experience of figuring out what the anomaly does and learning how to forget things in order to cope with it is genuine- but it isn't the first time he had that experience. He did it at least once before, but eventually reached a point where he didn't think he could cope anymore. At that point, he wrote a note to himself and deliberately forgot his experience in SCP-2503 so far. After that he had no memories of being in the anomaly before, so when he woke up, he concluded that he'd just arrived.

However, erasing his experience didn't restore things he'd already forgotten. Thus, right from the start of the journal, his memories are incomplete or incorrect, like his attempts to recall his roommate's name later on: he misidentifies his son, he misremembers what he did on the last pub night.

There are no multiversal or retrocausal shenanigans at work here- it's just that the only available narrator is one who's admitted to messing with his own head.

(I also think, based on the descriptions of his behavior prior to disappearing, that Henry put himself in SCP-2503. This may be part of what he decided to forget before he started the journal, and why the note that he found was worded to allow him to think someone else was responsible- knowing that it really is all your fault is an easy way to make a bad situation worse.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ???? Not a very helpful comment, Daioni… Could you elaborate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this to be uninteresting overall, the article itself is alright. But the journal was predictable and boring. It felt like a chore to finish reading the journal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the name of this shold be losing time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A cruel mind?

It can be a human (some humans can be very cruel, right? ;>), or it can be a monster, or some entity, or… I like that this is left to the reader's imagination.

There is the possibility that one (?) boss in the architecture company is responsible for the anomaly, through magic or advanced science or whatever, and he (?) seeks occasional victims among his employees.

Or some kind of Power that Be is responsible for it, and… same as above!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This doesn't really grip me. The bit at the beginning of the log where it seems that whoever did this to him thought they were doing him a favor was interesting, but the rest of the log was pretty standard as far as immortal-trapped-in-a-box horror goes.

If you were writing just for me, I'd suggest expanding on why they thought it would be a good thing for him. That is, what sort of mind sees someone who is stressed out over work and decides that the best solution is this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it in the Sandbox, and I still like it.
Not excellent, not perfect, but surely good enough for an upvote from me.

I agree with many of Kalinin's remarks: good advice on some choice of words and shortening sentences. But … I was not bored at all. To me, Henry is a consistent, in-depth character, whom I can admire and feel sorry for. I don't find the log 'narmy' - on the contrary, considering the context, I find the style quite sober… but that might be because Evrien edited it in the meantime?
I like the discreet touch of clues here and there, the final ambiguities (who is Henry? who put him there? etc.)… I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After doing some research on this and reading it over a few times, I decided "good enough for me"!
You earned yourself an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I checked out the similarity as noted also by Kalinin, but since I've never read the book I don't know to what extent this similarity exists. I just hope that it is not going to appear outright plagiarism.

And I thought about leaving the ending in a mystery with some hints, allowing multiple interpretations while leaving some room for the reader to think about the suspense. But I can definitely see where you are going at, but I do wish to preserve the current ending…perhaps install another addendum? Then my fear is that it would make the article too long in length.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi Kalinin,

First of all thanks a lot for the detailed feedback! In all honesty I did not expect such an in-depth review on the first day, especially not after having officially posted this. I posted this earlier in my sandbox and worked on it in the forums and because I received mostly positive feedback with stylistic discrepancies, I thought I am ready for submitting. I saw your reply when I was on train and I rushed back home to make the edits.

Here are my responses:

+ show block
– hide block

The "SCP" acronym thing appears a lot in older articles, but it makes absolutely no sense in-universe.

This was recommended to me by another member in the sandbox, and at the time I thought this was pretty smart and I guess I was a little high on life or something. After a sober review this does appear rather out-of-place and I have changed it to the original pseudonym.

In light of" is a little informal for what you're going for here … in the master bedroom.

All points taken and changes have been made as I deemed fit in this part.

This is another thing that happens a lot (so it's not really limited to you) but doesn't make sense to me. Why would knowing when something happened within a ten-year period be okay, but not the particular year? Why wouldn't you just blackbox the whole year if the date is privileged information?

How I interpreted blackboxing is that it's to hide certain details, while still providing enough background for the reader to gain at least a vague sense of understanding. A lot could happen in 10 years and because the discovery also sets the time of the journal's earliest entry, I want to at least provide this much information to the reader.

This is an extraneous sentence. … You can just say "currently."

All points taken and changes have been made as I deemed fit in this part.

——————Changes made above this line——————

Then it's not an experiment. Experiments are things that are designed and carried out purposefully to gather data under controlled conditions. While data can be gathered from accidental occurrences, it's poor terminology to refer to something like that as an experiment.

The truth is I too debated with myself on this when I wrote it, but I do find more reasoning in your argument and I will change this to an incident report.

The "god-forsaken" bit is where the narm starts creeping into this.

Will look into get rid of the narm problem within 24 hours. Already have a plan and I am contemplating the execution, but it's coming.

Why is that redacted?

This is completely my bad. Long story short: used blackbox instead of a marker when I wrote this and left it thinking I'll go back to edit it. It just escaped me when I worked through editing it for final draft.

Sort of a weird observation for a middle aged Canadian white guy in the suburbs to make.

Henry's setting is an educated elite white collar in Vancouver, a very modernized Canadian city in the west coast. The site's location is a suburban area in the Greater Vancouver region. It's like Manhattan and Brooklyn.

So he's modern enough to have hung out with people who've worked on stuff like drones before, but he's still speaking about it like it's an esoteric particle collider or some such? Weird style here.

The use and mentioning of drones was not popularized in North America until 2012, and considering this took place at least a decade earlier, and that Henry was involved in architecture not engineering, I would say his alienation is at least understandable.

He's young enough to have had a small child when he went in, but he should be dead within seven years? Huh?

The addendum is a collection of excerpts, and I used … to indicate omission. In here he is talking about had he counted the years he spent in the SCP. I suppose this is just how we interpreted this differently?

If I'm to buy that this guy has been changed by his experiences of continually walking for redacted-hundreds of years, he's going to need to sound a little bit different than this. This is the same character with some dialogue that's clearly trying to sound world-weary and insightful, but not really succeeding.

I've thought a lot about this when I wrote it. I came to the conclusion that without taking in new knowledge (as one would go about life in the ordinary world), the development of the character is limited to how he processes the knowledge he already has, which is why I went with the world-weary direction.

I don't think that's what "impressionable" means.

Yes, a horrible choice of wording. Thanks for pointing it out.

I also looked into the plot summary of House of Leaves and found the resemblance you spoke of. And please believe me when I say that I have never read of the book (only heard of its title), and the concept of this SCP was from a nightmare I had some years ago.

Thanks again for your feedback. Please check back tomorrow if you have time. I understand what I'm doing is already beyond what the official SCP section is meant for, and I will do my best to present a more refined version fitting to be posted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So similar to House of Leaves that I actually went to find my copy and start reading again.

I actually think this would be more interesting with a different twist or ending, just not a journal. While I didn't like the article much outside of it, it felt like it could have gone somewhere interesting. And while the place it did go is the next logical step in the story, I've already read this kind of thing before, and done better. If the twist was that they drilled through the walls and discovered that the path was intended to lead to something or other, I'd probably hold interest more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Surely you didn't miss the multiple posts to this thread over the past couple days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You should probably post on the forums first. That way you can make such changes, because the material on the main site is supposed to represent your final work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot of execution problems in this article.

+ show text
- hide text

acquired by the Foundation under the name of one Mr. Samuel Coleridge Peterson

I'm not quite at the point where I will downvote an article on sight for doing this. But I'm getting close. The "SCP" acronym thing appears a lot in older articles, but it makes absolutely no sense in-universe. Why on earth would a secret organization go out of its way to narrow down possible front entities from "anything in the world" to "things what spell SCP"? There is absolutely nothing to be gained from a practice like that, and it's needlessly risky. It's not just you that does this, but it's exceedingly silly.

In light of its proximity to the local community, parents, teachers, and local authorities of the surrounding area are advised to discourage children from accessing the premise of the containment site. Civilians are to be deterred from entering under the excuse of dangerous building conditions.

"In light of" is a little informal for what you're going for here. "Due to" would probably work better.

I'm not sure why you're focusing on discouraging children in particular from accessing this. And if you aim to do that, creating an urban legend (which is what you're going to end up doing with this) about the spooooky house that no one should go to ever is going to lead to the exact opposite of what you're intending.

"Excuse" is also informal. "Under the guise of," "using X as a pretext," "condemned by local authorities as unsafe," etc. etc. There's any number of ways to rephrase that in a more clinical manner. Also, you don't mention how people are to be "deterred". Really, this whole section could have been some variant of "put a fence up around it and have it condemned" and we could have called it a day. I don't usually pay much attention to containment procedures, but when it doesn't look well-developed enough, this section can drag articles down.

At all times, at least two security members for each of Site-2503’s entry points are to remain on site. Anyone who enters SCP-2503-1 without permission is considered lost if reappearance does not take place through SCP-2503-2 within 80 hours.

This seems excessive, and really why are you going to go to the trouble of the first part of your containment if the place is already under 24 hour armed guard with at least (I assume) four guys watching it all the time? And how many people are going into this thing without permission that it merits a mention in the procedures? And how are they sneaking past multiple guards all the time? None of this makes any sense to me.

located within a town community about ██ kilometers north of Burnaby, BC, Canada

"town community" doesn't sound right, you could probably just say "town" or "city" or whatever the political subdivision is. You can also just right "X kilometers north," since "about" sounds imprecise for the context you're using.

Site-2503 is a residential house

You can probably just say "residence". Or even "house". Minor point.

According to Site-2503’s blueprint, the room SCP-2503 inhabits is the master bedroom on the second floor, henceforth referred to as SCP-2503-1.

This is a little confusing; is the spatial anomaly actually indicated on the filed blueprints for the house? If it isn't, you can probably just say that the anomaly is located (not "inhabits" unless it really is a living thing) in the master bedroom.

The presence of SCP-2503 was discovered in 199█

This is another thing that happens a lot (so it's not really limited to you) but doesn't make sense to me. Why would knowing when something happened within a ten-year period be okay, but not the particular year? Why wouldn't you just blackbox the whole year if the date is privileged information?

Noteworthy is the nature of SCP-2503’s inner space

This is an extraneous sentence. There may be some justification for it in-universe (though "noteworthy" is a touch informal), but really, the reader should be able to figure out the fact that a crazy time-bending hallway being in this person's house is "noteworthy".

It is discovered that time and space operate differently within SCP-2503.

"It is discovered" is clumsy phrasing. You can just say that time and space operate differently in the anomaly, though probably in a more clinical manner than that.

Objects that enter SCP-2503 do not age while inside SCP-2503's space but will nonetheless experience a sense of time flow. Due to this nature, the object’s real time “freezes” while inside SCP-2503, and the speed with which it travels within SCP-2503 does not influence the amount of time it experiences.

"Sense of time flow" doesn't really mean anything, and the scare-quoted "freezes" is something that wouldn't show up in a clinical document. "Subjects will perceive the passage of time", "the subject does not experience the actual passage of time", phrases like this are more what the context demands.

At the current point, SCP-2503-2 is the only known exit of SCP-2503-1.

"At the current point" is unnecessary and is also slightly weird phrasing. You can just say "currently."

This experiment was carried out by accident

Then it's not an experiment. Experiments are things that are designed and carried out purposefully to gather data under controlled conditions. While data can be gathered from accidental occurrences, it's poor terminology to refer to something like that as an experiment.

The note is a bit of a different creature, and so my observations are mostly stylistic at this point:

you may demand some monetary reward for your service.

This seemed strangely formal and out of place considered with his plea to take the message to his family. Not necessarily an error of writing, but it scanned weird to me when I read it.

The reason I am writing is to prepare for the worst case scenario and to record down details of my misfortune.

"Record down" is an odd choice of phrase from what I imagine a highly-educated person like this would sound like. This sentence also seems more like something I'd see in an essay, rather than a last message to loved ones.

I should probably begin with how I ended up in my current state

Again, this is a little formal, and it conjures up thousands of internet creepypastas with the whole "it all started when" thing.

Nonetheless, your case may be different, and so here’s my story if you wouldn’t mind keep reading.

This sentence doesn't scan properly with the "wouldn't mind keep reading" and again, it sounds like something typed up on NoSleep. I mean, he's imagining that someone may come along and find this note after ending up in the same inexplicable situation that he's in. Is he really worred about that hypothetical person finding a note that may pertain to their situation, and then going "boooring" and just tossing it aside?

At some point, I just blacked out completely, but before that I do remember asking Will and Kevin to get me home in case I get too shitfaced. It now looks like they didn’t do as they were asked.

Notes of realism and minor details are what make a piece feel real to a reader and convince them to suspend their disbelief. In this case, no. I'm in a field where alcohol is pretty much a job requirement, and getting blackout drunk at work functions is not something that people really do. If they do get to that point, it's because they have some profound problem and they're about to self-destruct. Your character does not come across as such a person, and if they were drugged or something like that, they would probably have noticed that that was unusual for them to not remember what happened the night before and remarked upon that. Stuff like this is preventing me from believing this person as a real character.

There’s also no way to tell time in this god-forsaken place

The "god-forsaken" bit is where the narm starts creeping into this.

My instinct is telling me to go towards the light (let’s hope that isn’t the last bad pun I pull).

That's…not a pun so much as a garden-variety joke, and also this is really dissonant in terms of the tone that you're trying to set here.

So I suppose it’s time to write down another entry. Unlike the previous ██ times

Why is that redacted?

I’ve been purposely ignoring that 9927 in fear of this…IN FEAR OF THIS!

Narm intensifies. He's really repeating "in fear of this" again and bolding and underlining it in a written account?

But I am no God – hell I’m not even a sadhu or a sage!

Sort of a weird observation for a middle aged Canadian white guy in the suburbs to make.

I haven’t seen one myself, but think it is what you call a “drone.” It’d be one of those things Ji Da that one guy who I roomed with during my sophomore year in college (it’s been too long) – he’d be all thrilled about it 'cause he’s in engineering.

So he's modern enough to have hung out with people who've worked on stuff like drones before, but he's still speaking about it like it's an esoteric particle collider or some such? Weird style here.

Can’t believe it has been 7 years since my last entry. I have become inert. By age, I should have died.

He's young enough to have had a small child when he went in, but he should be dead within seven years? Huh?

I felt nostalgic and overwhelmed with emotions - anger and sadness, hope and despair, a grim pessimism coupled with an ironic optimism.

If I'm to buy that this guy has been changed by his experiences of continually walking for redacted-hundreds of years, he's going to need to sound a little bit different than this. This is the same character with some dialogue that's clearly trying to sound world-weary and insightful, but not really succeeding.

I don’t know. I feel disgusted with what my immediate reaction was. It was neither grief nor angst, but avarice.

That makes very little sense. Why would this guy, supposedly so detached from self and humanity that he can look back on his former writings and be reminded of a person he used to be, who has no material needs since he doesn't need to eat or drink or rest, feel the need to scavenge stuff as his immediate reaction?

I’m writing this with only so little sanity left within me …and I must admit that I'm approaching the border of madness.

I don't necessarily buy into "show not tell" as a commandment of writing. But if you're trying to portray a character that is losing their mind, surely there are more artful ways of doing that than straight-up having him say "I only have a little bit of sanity left". This strikes me more as someone's PC from a Call of Cthuhlu game than a literary character.

Upon this path I walk alone and now only to become blind to all but solitude.

You might be able to get away with this line with proper character development and context. Maybe. Here it's just making me cringe.

but it’s too impressionable to be written off like that

I don't think that's what "impressionable" means.

The journal, upon recovery, was luckily in good condition

"Luckily" is poor tone since we're now back into clinical documentation.

The amount of lapses in execution in this would have been plenty on their own to earn the downvote from me. But generally speaking, this suffers from a couple of larger issues as well.

Mostly, it's boring me. I attribute that to the fact that the bulk of your story is this guy who's trapped in the time/space anomaly, but for the reasons elucidated above, he's not coming across to me as a believable character. I don't buy him as a person, and so his struggles and his fate don't really affect me. You had quite a lot of text to use to characterize him, but instead this just felt like a slog.

Also, whether it's intended or not, this treads very closely to the portion of House of Leaves right before the climax. Before anyone takes this out of context, no, I don't think it's plagiarism or anything close, but to anyone who's read the book, this comparison would (I imagine) come immediately to mind, and this does not compare favorably to the writing in that book. That's more a personal reaction of mine as I read this. Doesn't really factor into my vote, but it did affect my reading experience.

This isn't unsalvageable, but this is far closer to first draft than finished product. There is a lot of work to be done here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the Marty/Marianne thing is clearly a clue, but I am not sure to what. Did he emerge in an alternate universe from the one he left?

Edit: No really, can someone please explain what the deal with the kids is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, I'm more impressed at how fast this got a reply. Thank you for your input. I will continue to work in these areas over the next few days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not terrible. The writing could be better though, especially in the journal segments, and several places could be cleaned up in the containment procedures and description. It definitely does need some work, though. The journals can go from typical "descent into insanity" to plain old narm. I exit without any real feel for this guy beyond him going insane. He's just generic going insane guy, who at times tends towards wordy exposition and cliché.

Overall, I'll novote for now, but may come back to downvote later when I'm feeling less kind. The only thing really preventing my downvote is the general feel that my issues can be fixed.
